content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
February 10, 1997
Gene Raper
Russo Marsh & Raper, Inc.
770 L Street, Suite 950
Post Office Box 1863
Sacramento, California 95812
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-053
Dear Mr. Raper:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Since you are not asking as the authorized representative of an
individual whose duties are in question, we are treating this as a request for informal advice
only.
2 (Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329(b)(2)(A).)
QUESTION
May a political party accept an unlimited contribution from a person ifthat contribution
is designated for party building activities, eg., voter registration?
CONCLUSION
No. Section 85303 limits the amount a political party may accept from any person to
$5,000 per calendar year. There is no exception to this contribution limit other than the one
expressly provided in Sections 85303 and 89519 that allow a candidate to distribute any surplus,
residual, or unexpended campaign funds to a political party committee
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-053
Page 2
ANALYSIS
Section 85303 limits the amount that may be contributed to political parties:
“No person shall give in the aggregate to political party committees
ofthe same political party, and no such party committees ofthe
same political party, and no such party committees combined shall
accept from any person, a contribution or contributions totaling
more than five thousand dollars ($5,000) per calendar year; except
a candidate may distribute any surplus, residual, or unexpended
campaign funds to a political party committee.” (Section 85303.)
Section 82015 defmes a “contribution”:
“‘Contribution’ means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a
payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to
make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear from the circumstances
that it is not made for political purposes.” (Section 82015.)
Regulation 18215 interprets Section 82015:
(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. A
payment is made for political purposes if it is:
(1) For the purposes of influencing or attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any
measure; or
(2) Received by or made at the behest ofthe following or any agent
thereof:
(A) A candidate;
(B) A controlled committee;
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state
central committee. country central committee, assembly district
committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or
***
(Regulation 18215. Emphasis added.)


File No. 1-97-053
Page 3
Therefore, a payment received by a political party is a contribution subject to the
contribution limits of Section 85303, except as specifiedthere. (Also see Section 89519(b).)
Regulation 18215 does provide exceptions to the defmition of contribution. However,
none exempt contributions made for political party building activities, e.g., voter registration.
(Regulation 18215 enclosed.)
The Commission has in the past exempted voter registration efforts from the purview of
the Act. (In re Welsh (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 71.) However, those voter registration drives were
found to be non-partisan efforts. A voter registration drive has been found non-partisan because:
(1) The program was not controlled by any candidate or partisan
organization or committee;
(2) The program was designed to register voters irrespective of
party or political views;
(3) Any services were offered to any organization; and,
(4) Any solicitation of funds was not directed at candidates or
organizations of a particular political party.
(In re Welsh (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By: Marte Castafios
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:MC:ak
